text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac widely, followed by dissection along the lower border of the pancreas and the adjacent mass.

The surgeon dissects a large cystic mass of mucinocystic tumor that has replaced the body and tail of the pancreas. The mass is adherent to the transverse muscle column, proximal jejunal loops, and duodenal flexure, and these adhesions are being carefully separated.
The surgeon leaves a portion of the mesocolon attached to the cystic mass to prevent rupture and spillage of cyst contents during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly towards the retroperitoneum, carefully separating the tissue layers to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the loose areolar tissue plate in the retroperitoneum, revealing its distinct texture and location relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon proceeds to identify the superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and splenic vein, carefully examining these vascular structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially, carefully separating the cystic mass from adjacent structures such as the transverse muscle column, proximal jejunal loops, and duodenal flexure.
The surgeon exposes the junction of the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein, highlighting the anatomical connection between these vascular structures.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac further towards the duodenum, enhancing the visual field for better access and visibility of the surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon dissects behind the neck of the pancreas along the superior mesenteric portal vein axis. The splenic vein appears stretched and arched due to the size of the cyst, requiring careful manipulation.

The surgeon identifies small tributaries draining into the veins and proceeds to clip and divide the splenic vein. The stem of the splenic vein is observed to be very short in relation to the portal vein, requiring careful application of clips.
The surgeon divides the neck of the pancreas due to the presence of a large cyst, which obscures the splenic artery, making it difficult to identify or dissect.
The surgeon prepares to divide the neck of the pancreas and further dissect the splenic artery, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
After dividing the neck of the pancreas, the surgeon identifies anatomical structures in the surgical field, focusing on the area around the splenic artery and vein.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery, applies clips to secure it, and then divides the artery.

The surgeon performs further dissection towards the body and tail of the pancreas in the retroperitoneum, carefully separating the spleen and the entire specimen from the surrounding retroperitoneal tissue.
The surgeon divides the final short gastric vessels, completing the separation of these vessels from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon places the dissected specimen into a surgical retrieval bag for removal from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon places small, fine stitches in the surgical field, likely to secure or close tissue layers following dissection.
The surgeon delivers the specimen, which has been fully dissected and prepared for removal.
Thank you.
